  
**Class Structure**

It’s important to adhere to a basic format on how the structure is taught.  Curriculum is as important, and is covered elsewhere, but if there isn’t a systematic infrastructure on how the material is delivered, your results will not be as consistent.  There is a fair amount of research done on how humans learn new skills, so it would make sense to apply those techniques to the bjj curriculum.

The research has clearly shown that a greater amount of learning occurs when students are motivated and passionate about what they are learning.  By cultivating an environment where your students are engaged and challenged, yet having fun, you are in turn cultivating an environment where maximum learning can take place.  This comes before we even consider technical content and the delivery systems which will be covered later.

For children in the tots program (4-7), a 30-45 minute class is most appropriate.  As they get older, you can push to a 1 hour class for the youth program which is set for ages 8-13.  As you move to the longer class structure, more drilling and sparring can be included, as well as additional technical content, with the mat chats and games taking the same amount of time.

Maintaining a firm, but not rigid class structure is critical in maintaining order and control of the class.  Children will continually test boundaries and it is vital to establish what is and what isn’t acceptable behavior in the class.  If a child shows up late, make them wait on the edge of class until you allow them to join the class.  If they misbehave, exclude them from the class for a period of time until you allow them to rejoin.  On the flip side, highlight the child that is showing the most desirable behavior and reward them.  This shows the other kids what you want to see and the class will mold them.  

If you witness a student doing something well, take the opportunity to showcase their abilities in front of the class during a circle up moment.  This sets the tone of what you are looking for as the coach/professor and gives the other students motivation and something to aspire to.  Children reach extremely well to positive reinforcement which is a powerful tool to influence the behaviors we wish to see on the mats.  
The head instructor of the class needs to be focused on delivering the technical content of the class as well as keeping the class on track and on schedule.  Utilize your assistant coaches to jump in and help with students that are struggling, talking out of turn, or otherwise misbehaving, as small infractions can spread into larger issues that disrupt the entire flow of the class if the main instructor is continually interrupted.

**The general outline for ages in each of the kids programs is listed below:**

* Tots class:	Ages 4 \- 7   
* Youth class: 	Ages 8 \- 13

Kids will be assessed individually to measure suitability for each program.  The ages are used as guidelines only and may be adjusted as necessary

**Class structure times are:**

* 30 minutes for the Tots class  
* 60 mins for the Youth class

The above times are selected based on the attention span, work ethic, and energy levels of the participants in each program.  Again, each student is different but these times have proven to work best for the instructor, the students, and the program.

**Basic overview of a Youth Class:  60 mins**  
Below is an example of a typical class structure.  This template can be used as a rough guide.

* Bow in, announcements, welcome new students  
  * Short as possible  
* Warm up.  (Fundamental/topic specific movements)  
  * Focus on fundamental movements and basic skill development  
* Takedown lesson   
  * Short, concise material.  2 x 2-3 min lessons  
  * Add additional content for senior student groups   
* Takedown drill   
  * 2 x 3 min   
  * Specific to the class lesson	  
* Ground technique lesson   
  * Short, concise material.  2 x 2-3 min lessons  
  * Add additional content for senior student groups   
* Ground technique drilling   
  * Positional/goal based drilling.  Specific to class material  
* Positional/Open sparring   
  * Target ⅓ of the class  
* Bow out/Student of the day/Strip promotions

Each lesson section should be no longer than 3 minutes, but I will break the lesson into two sections:  One to show the technique and to let the students attempt it. And two, to observe and call back to correct common mistakes.  The remainder of time is used to set up drills, break out groups, call ups, group resets, etc.  Keep this time to a minimum and ensure that the students respond quickly to your requests otherwise it will throw off the pace of the class.  I cannot stress this enough.

**Roll Call**

Highest ranking student line up left to right

Announce any additions to the class and introduce any new students.  Make sure to use their name and have everyone give them a round of applause for trying a class.  Our job is to make them feel as welcome as possible.  Once they have signed up, “promote” them to white belt.  Yes, this is a promotion and statistically speaking, the hardest belt to get.  Positively preframe the class by saying something like “You guys ready to have some fun?” or “Who wants to get closer to becoming a black belt?”

**Warm ups**

The goal of the warm up is simply to set the tone and structure for the class, incorporate new students into the group, and to get the students used to moving their bodies in fundamental methods.

Give the opportunity to assistant coaches for the honor of running the warm up if possible.  This helps them to be comfortable in front of a group and to find their own “coach voice”.  Keep the warm up short, (approximately 5 mins), energy high, and moves relevant to the course material covered during the week where possible.  (For example, If teaching double leg takedowns, have the students practice duck walks)

This is also the best time to get the students to watch and emulate the fundamental movements of jiujitsu (ie-shrimping, bridging, breakfalls, etc) without having to spend specific time teaching them one on one if you don’t have a separate intro class. 

**Life Skills Chat**  
One of the goals in creating a top quality kids martial arts program is to focus on developing the students' foundational skills and values that will help them to grow as individuals.  Much like building a house, a strong foundation based on a value system that includes traits such as respect, discipline, perseverance, humility, restraint, and loyalty, etc, will help the child to develop into future stewards not only for the art of jiujitsu, but for the community as a whole.  We use the art of brazilian jiu jitsu as a muse to help guide these kids into the absolute best versions of themselves. 

When most people think of martial arts, they often think only of physical capacity, but we are teaching students through a holistic approach to life that develops not only the body, but the mind and spirit as well.  This way, our students are physically strong, mentally flexible, and emotionally stable. This should be the ultimate objective of any martial arts kids program.

Use this portion where appropriate.  Discussions are age dependent.  Use this time to let students earn a game break through good attention and answering questions.  Preframe this section of class by “Who wants to play \_\_\_\_\_\_?”  Okay, first let’s answer a few questions.

**So what is a life skill?  What do those words mean?**  

Here we are looking to reinforce and affirm skills that align with the goals and value systems contained in the core of the program.  A brief list of examples below of topics you can discuss and ask them questions on:

* **Facing challenges in your life** (Be it school or elsewhere. Remind them it’s ok to be scared, and true courage stems from facing our fears)  
* **Overcoming adversity**  (What challenge did you overcome?  Did you pass a hard test?)  
* **Positive mindset** (Ask questions about positive things that happened recently. We strive to limit the negative content we consume and focus on the positive experiences in our lives.  This helps them develop an attitude of gratitude\!)  
* **Helping others** (Who have you helped recently?  Someone at school or at home?)  
* **Striving for excellence** (striving for excellence involves failing… again and again.  Discuss the importance of not fearing failure.  Instead, look to seek it, to embrace it, as it’s the key to growth.  
* **Authenticity and integrity** (what does this mean to you? Can you discuss a time where you were pressured to conform to something you did not agree with?  Discuss the importance of having a personal code to live by and sticking to it in the face of adversity.  Always be yourself\!  
* **Show respect** (Why is this important?  Treat others the way you want to be treated.  Showing respect to your elders/teachers/parents, etc)

**Games**

Typically, I will leave the actual games until Friday, and usually near the beginning of class, but fun drills with specific challenges can be added anywhere and are extremely fun and motivating for the kids.  (For example, instead of simply getting the kids to simply drill a technique, challenge them to do as many clean reps that they can during the allowable time and make them remember their number.  When they announce their numbers, high five the winner and note them as today’s student of the day.)   Specific games themselves should mostly be BJJ specific.  Elsewhere I have included a list of games with jiujitsu spins on them.  [Sample BJJ games](https://docs.google.com/document/d/193DXK_zIsjSeW_aYq6yWAZWTCHY6oIolEfVr9k_e-fY/edit)

**Takedown/Ground technical lesson**

When a new technique is being taught to a class, whether it be a takedown or a ground specific technique, it should be delivered in the following manner:  This model applies to the adult program as well.

* Demonstrate the technique in real time (This helps focus and excite the team to learn\!)  
* Demonstrate the technique slowly and breakdown each movement (keep specifics short, and focus on the main movements.  Small adjustments and details can be added later.  Showing every detail all at once will confuse the students in the short term)  
* Show the technique no more than 3 times  
* Allow the students to try the technique for 1 round  
* Bring the students back to correct common mistakes  
* Students drill the technique again under minimal resistance  
* Drill again under progressive resistance model


  

Having a lesson plan prepared prior to the class is a useful tool for instructors, especially ones that are just starting out.  Keeping a list handy of talking points and timelines for each section will assist you with maintaining the flow of the class.  Study the lesson plan prior to class and print it off or have it available on your phone to reference quickly between teaching sections.  Do not feel bad about needing this when you are new to instructing.  It shows a commitment to becoming a better coach and over time, you will need it less and less.

I always make sure to ask the students questions surrounding the subject matter to ensure that they are paying attention and are understanding the material.  Engage them as part of the conversation and they will surprise you in many different ways, including the way they conceptualize the lesson.  There is much to learn from the way children process material that will make you a better teacher if you are listening to what they are saying.  Do not be afraid to be challenged by them.  
**Drilling**

Proper drilling is one of the most impactful pieces of your jiu jitsu training.  It bridges the gap between technical demonstration and open sparring.  Although many clubs still stick to outdated training methodologies (whereby instruction is followed by sparring), the vast majority of world class gyms dedicate a large portion of their training to specific drilling techniques.  By using a progressive overload model, any student can train at their own pace and slowly increase the speed and intensity of their training over time, while simultaneously being challenged and developing specific areas of their game.

Conceptually, we are seeking to hone in on specific areas of our game with focused based drills to develop strong muscle memory responses and synaptic transmissions.  As we execute the same move multiple times, over 1000’s of repetitions, we are reinforcing the motor memory from the short term encoding phase to the more stable, long term memory consolidation phase.  At this phase, the movements become as natural to us as walking.

These drills can also force you to work on your weaker areas of your game and in turn, help develop them into strengths in ways that would not be possible if you spent all your time sparring.  Open sparring is great and essential, but it’s typical when rolling that students will play their “A” game and avoid positions that they aren’t as strong in; thus never developing those key knowledge areas.  This is especially true with the childrens classes although it does hold true for all ages.

There are multiple solo drills that you can do on your own to help with certain movements, flexibility, etc, however, the majority of our training should be done with a partner as it is difficult to replicate a technique effectively without a partner.

Examples of specific drilling methods include:

**Sport specific drills:**   

Drills that are unique to bjj and specific to the lesson plan of the week.  The majority of our drilling will be based on this.

* **Solo drills**: specific movements that can be done anytime, without a partner.  They can be beneficial to develop movement patterns through repetition.  We mainly utilize these during our warmup.  Don’t be afraid to think outside the box, get creative, and combine movements to make it more exciting and challenging for students.  
  Some examples include:  
  * Break falls: Forward roll, front, side, rear  
  * Bridging:  Side to Side, Bridge to turtle,   
  * Sit out:  Turtle to sit out  
  * Core drills:  Boat rocks, solo triangles  
  * Takedown drills:  duck walks, sprawls, foot sweeps  
  * Guard drills:  butt scoot, triangles, hip drops, inverts  
  * Scramble drills:  sit outs, back steps, bear crawls, technical get up  
  * And more……  
    

The list below discusses examples of types of drills that can be used as baselines for assembling effective drill methods based on course curriculum:

* **Call out drills:**  Instructor students to begin in a specific position and “calls out” the move they want to see executed.  An example would be starting the students in side control, and calling out the next position you want them to get to.  
* **Pocket drills:**  Instructor gets students to start in a position and remain there for the round.  These drills are effective at developing skill sets in a specific area. An example would be to start in the closed guard position where the top player must defend, but cannot pass.  They must stay in the “pocket”.  
* **Objective drills:**  Start in a position where you must achieve an objective.  Example would be to submit with a certain submission, or student must score 6 points  
* **Reset drills:**  Pick a goal to achieve.  If the student accomplishes the goal, then reset and start again.  Examples would include a passing drill where students begin in open guard, if the top player passes, then reset.




**UKE vs TORI \- What does that mean?**  
When drilling, it is equally important to be a good “uke” as it is to be a good “tori”.  The former refers to the person who is having the technique executed on them, whereas the latter refers to the person executing the technique.  A good uke is essential if you expect to have an effective training session.  They should replicate realistic movement patterns that you would experience during a roll, but at a reduced speed and resistance, at least initially.  

As the students become more confident in the technique, resistance can be dialed up to even the level of an open sparring round, but the scope of the roll remains within the pocket of the drill.  The instructor should set a baseline of the resistance they would like to see based on the goals of the drill.  Children understand a “dial” reference, such as 1-10 or 0%-100%, much better than non-specific instructions such as “go light”.  Some students will require more time to “adjust” their dial, so make adjustments with the individual students if they are wavering from the rules laid out in the drill.

As the instructor, it is also your job to ensure that students understand that there are two parts to every drill, not to have one person drilling, and one person doing nothing.  Like yin and yang, there is a balance between both sides and a team effort is required if the training group is to have a solid drilling session.  As this tends to be the most difficult part of the class for students to retain focus, setting specific instructions with round timers or call outs keeps the students engaged and makes it more fun.  A little competition during drilling can also add to the excitement:  games like “king/queen of the mat” are a prime example.

**Highlight students**

If you witness a student doing something well during a drilling session, take the opportunity to highlight and showcase their abilities in front of the class during a circle up moment.  This sets the tone of what you are looking for as the coach/professor and gives the other students motivation and something to aspire to.  Children react extremely well to positive reinforcement which can be used as a powerful tool to influence the behaviors we wish to see on the mats.

**Sparring**

Sparring is a critical component for skill acquisition in bjj, and simultaneously one of the most fun and rewarding.  This is the opportunity for students to open up the game and integrate all the tools they have learned to date.  It’s also a time for them to explore the depths of their depths of jiu jitsu, to try new things, and sharpen their sword on techniques that they are proficient in.

Make sure to pair up students with partners that you feel are most appropriate.  Each class will vary, as will the skill set, experience, age, and sizes within the group.  If there are no even matches, try pairing up a more senior, smaller student, with a larger, more junior student to balance out the match.  

Other, less visible considerations for matchmaking are traits like emotional regulation, temperament, and personality meshing.  If two students cannot seem to get along or train effectively together, it will not matter if they are similarly sized or skilled.  This is rare, but does happen from time to time.  Our job is to ensure that they are challenged, but are also having a positive experience.  This is critical for growth to occur.

The focus of this portion of class should initially be on positional sparring based on the course material of the week.  Ideally, we are looking for a minimum of 3 drilling rounds before proceeding to open sparring.

Leave time for at least 2-3, 3 min open sparring matches to prepare the students for what a competition style match will look like.  Students with at least 1 stripe on their belts can start from standing and roll to submission based on the coaches approval.  New white belt students should stick to positional sparring until they understand the basics of movement, breakfalling, and tapping to submissions.  Once you are comfortable in their ability to open spar, allow them to do so, as long as they clearly understand how/when to tap, and when to release a submission.  

**Competition training sessions**  
This class is offered as an invite only initiative to students wanting to compete.  Students must understand that the competition training sessions are higher intensity than the regular class with a heavier focus on sparring, both positional and open, and want to participate.

The focus of this class is to mentally and physically prepare the students for the increased intensity of a competition and to sharpen the tools in their disposal.  Children should be much more self motivated here and if they are wasting time, do not invite them back until they can prove themselves in the regular class. 

 As an example, a competition style training class can be structured as below to include:

* **BJJ specific warmup:** Example: 20 triangles each followed by 20 armbars from guard each  
* **Discussion on rules and strategy**:  Example: cover IBJJF point system.  Discuss strategies such as being first and getting on top and staying on top, etc.  
* **Review one takedown:**  Can be tailored to the individual competing  
* **Drill a couple rounds takedown only:**  Reset once it hits the ground and position is secured  
* **Review one ground scenario:**  Example: you are on bottom and losing on points, you have 1 minute to score or submit  
* **Drill specific scenarios:**  Multiple rounds, different partners, or shark bait, etc  
* **Open sparring rounds:**  3 minute rounds starting on the feet.  Vary the partners if possible  
* **Mat chat to finish and answer questions**

Take steps to tailor the class to the students who are competing and ruleset they are competing under.

**General physical preparedness drills (GPP)** 

These drills are used less often on the mats during the regular class but are a must for the competition team.  Injury prevention and preparedness go hand in hand.  These drills help prepare the body for the repetitive strain we put on our bodies during hard bjj sparring sessions.  As with any sport, certain muscle groups and joints are overused, where others are underused  which can lead to imbalance within the body and lead to injury.  Some examples of types of drills that can be incorporated into your lesson plan or personal training routines are listed below.

* **Stability drills:**  Ensuring that isolate and develop underutilized muscles  
* **Mobility drills:**  Ensuring that we have effective range of motion as well as command of the muscle throughout its range of motion.  
* **Flexibility drills:**  Ensuring that students have effective range of motion to meet the flexibility demands of BJJ  
* **Strength drills:**  Weight training is not recommended or required for kids, but children will gain sufficient strength from bodyweight movements such as frog jumps.  
* **Speed/agility drills:**  Pushing the envelope to increase these traits without the expense of clean technique execution.  
* **Coordination drills:**  Training the mind to strengthen the mind/body connection, increase reaction time, etc

**Mat Safety Program**

It goes without saying that safety on the mats is paramount.  The parents of our students are entrusting us with a huge honor and responsibility:  the safety of their children.  Now in saying that, jiu jitsu is a combat sport that can be tough, and small injuries from time to time are somewhat inevitable.  Our goal is to minimize the risks whenever possible by having our coaches trained in mat safety protocols.  The premise of a safe training environment are detailed below:

* Having sufficient coaches and assistant coaches on the mats who are continually scanning the mats for concerns like emotional escalations, holding submissions, or crowding.  
* At least one coach on the mats needs to be trained in first aid  
* All coaches must know where the first aid kit is located and where the muster points are in the event of a fire.  
* Teaching the students that they are individually responsible for their partners safety.   Ask them who the most important person in the gym is?  The answer is:  Your training partner.  Have them repeat this.  
* Reinforcing the standard that we are not promoting based on how many students you have submitted.  Use mat chats to detail the difference between training hard and training recklessly. We want to train hard, but not at the expense of safety.    
* Teaching students the techniques necessary to physically protect themselves and to make them more durable:  As an example:  how to properly breakfall when being thrown  
* The tap must **ALWAYS** be honored and students must understand this clearly. Always take time to preface the sparring session with a chat about spatial awareness, how and when to tap, and how to safely apply submissions.  
* Limiting exposures to takedowns and submissions with new students until they are prepared.  Typically we permit this at 1 stripe on their white belt.




**Community**

Community building is an important pillar of any accomplished martial arts program that wants to retain its viability long term. There is a misconception that bjj is an individual sport and while we may compete individually within a match, we train together as a team, support each other as a team, and represent the team.  Each person you train with has an impact on you and who you become, so that in that sense, on the mats, we are all one.  Fun, team building exercises and games are easy to include into the regular class, but engaging one another outside of the mats is important so that students continue to feel engaged and continue to build and maintain those lifelong friendships.

**Circle up/Announcements/Promotions**

Keep announcements simple, speaking to parents as well as students.  Discuss upcoming promotion events, tournaments, team events, etc as they arise.  Award **move of the day** and **student of the day (SOTD)** based more on positive behavior such as paying attention, asking good questions, working hard during class, etc and less on performance.  

If there is a new student trying the class for the first time, I take that opportunity to highlight that student as the SOTD and mention “that the first day at jiu jitsu is the hardest” and that it’s always challenging to try something new. 

Kids will receive one stripe for (approximately) every 20 classes.  The Head coach (black belt) will give belt promotions.  Promotions for the kids program are based on a combination of the 4 “A” model: **A**ttendance, **A**bility, **A**ttitude, **A**ssistance (helping others).  Parents should be notified 

in advance for promotions so that they can attend and take photos.  For belt promotions, students will be sent home with a notice to promote form to be filed out by their parents and students.  The form will ask questions around behavior and work ethic at home and in the classroom.  This highlights our commitment in helping to grow respectable, dedicated, well adjusted students both on and off the mats. [Promotion form](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ZqY2uN0ZSBWU0YkJXF8iKsSZWd_hXIcuXwtN3kAUkJk/edit#heading=h.c6lr2da6yrwb)
